rulings.Case Title: IN RE: EXPRESS MESSENGER SYSTEMS, INC. DBA ONTRAC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Express Messenger System Inc.'S Petition To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 14, Express Messenger System Inc.'S Petition To Compel Arbitration. Hearing required. In particular, the parties shall be prepared at the hearing to address the following questions: 1. Do Respondent Jose Barboza Frazao Junior's claims in his complaint filed with the Department of Industrial Relations, Labor Commissioner's Office and set for hearing before the Department of Labor Standards Enforcement ("DLSE") fall within the "Limitations on Application of This Arbitration Provision" provision of the Non-Disclosure and Dispute Resolution Agreement (NDRA) between the parties, which provides in pertinent part, "Claims may be brought before, and remedies awarded by, an administrative agency if applicable law permits access to such an agency notwithstanding the existence of this Agreement."? 2. How is Petitioner Express Messenger Systems, Inc. d/b/a OnTrac's petition to compel arbitration of Respondent Frazao's complaint and request for a stay of DLSE proceedings affected by the five consolidated appeals involving OnTrac pending before the Court of Appeal? (Express Messenger Systems, Inc. v. Medeiros, Nos. A155085, A155086, A155092, A155093, A155095.) 3. In view of the temporary stay of DLSE proceedings ordered by the Court of Appeal on February 28, 2019 in those appeals pending its disposition of petitions for writ of supersedeas filed by OnTrac, does the DLSE intend to proceed with Respondent Frazao's DLSE hearing on March 6? 4. Does Petitioner contend that Code of Civil Procedure section 1281.4 empowers the Court to stay the DLSE hearing?=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JAYDEN BELL VS. BING CHEN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Re-Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Plaintiff To Respond To Supplemental Demand For Production And Inspection And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 5, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANTS BING CHEN, TONG YU'S Motion For Order Compelling Plaintiff To Respond To Supplemental Demand For Production And Inspection And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Melinda Derish, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' unopposed motion is granted. Sanctions are awarded against plaintiff in the amount of $360, to be paid by April 5, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to mtderish@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MIGUELITO BONIFACIO ET AL VS. FCA US, LLC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Attorney Fees, Costs And Expenses; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF MIGUELITO BONIFACIO, SEZZE BONIFACIO's Motion For Attorney Fees, Costs And Expenses. Plaintiffs Miguelito Bonifacio and Sezze Bonifacio's motion for attorney fees, costs, and expenses is granted in the amount of $17,714.50 in fees and $3,143.96 in costs and expenses. Plaintiffs' counsel unnecessarily and unreasonably expended 16.1 hours in preparing responses to written discovery after the parties had reached substantial agreement on the terms of their settlement, and 11.3 hours traveling to San Francisco and appearing at a January 15, 2019 hearing on an order to show cause that could have been avoided, had Plaintiffs dismissed the action within 45 days after they filed a Notice of Settlement of Entire Case on July 18, 2018, in which Plaintiffs represented that a request for dismissal would be filed no later than November 15, 2018. After subtracting those hours, the Court finds that a reasonable lodestar for counsel's work on the case is $17,714.50. The Court declines to award a multiplier. (See Hall v. FCA US LLC (E.D. Cal. May 21, 2018) 2018 WL 2298431 at *8 [declining to award multiplier in Song-Beverly Act case where, among other things, case did not present novel or difficult questions of law or fact, there was no evidence that the nature of the litigation precluded other employment by the attorneys, and the contingent nature of the fee award was outweighed by the other factors].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NATIONAL UNION FIRE INSURANCE COMPANY OF VS. AMERICAN ZURICH INSURANCE COMPANY AN ILLINOIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Action, Or, In The Alternative, Motion To Stay All Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 8, DEFENDANT ARUP NORTH AMERICA LTD.'s Motion To Stay Action, Or, In The Alternative, Motion To Stay All Discovery. Defendant and cross-complainant Arup North America Ltd.'s motion to stay entire action is denied. (See GGIS Insurance Services, Inc. v. Superior Court (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1493, 1505 [stay of coverage action not warranted where coverage issues can be decided as a matter of law without making factual determinations that would prejudice the insured in the third-party action]. Arup's alternative motion to stay discovery is granted. (See Riddell, Inc. v. Superior Court (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 755, 764-771; Haskel v. Superior Court (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 963, 980.) The Court finds that Zurich's discovery regarding the scope and character of Arup's work on the underlying projects is logically related to issues affecting liability in the underlying actions. (See, e.g., Zurich Special Interrogatories Nos. 3, 6, 7, 9, 10; Requests for Admission Nos. 3, 4, 7, 8, 9.) Therefore, a stay of discovery is appropriate. Zurich fails to show that a confidentiality order would be sufficient to protect Arup's interests. (See Riddell, 14 Cal.App.5th at 768, 770.) Zurich's claim of prejudice rings hollow. National Union correctly notes that Zurich was either aware of the allegations and extrinsic facts it needed to deny coverage at the time of the tender or it was not; it does not need discovery from Arup to defend National Union's motions. (See Montrose Chemical Corp. v. Superior Court (1993) 6 Cal.4th 287, 295; Haskel, 33 Cal.App.4th at 977.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KAYLA JENNINGS ET AL VS. CLIFFORD ROSS MOCK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admission
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 5, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFFs KAYLA JENNINGS, MICHAEL JOHNSON Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admission (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Melinda Derish, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required for parties to address whether the case is still on the trial calendar. If the case has not been resolved plaintiff's motion will be granted in part. Under CCP section 1124.24, plaintiff has demonstrated good cause to complete discovery limited to Requests for Admission (Set One), timely served prior to arbitration. The majority of defendant's objections were not justifiable and responses not Code-compliant. Defendant's argument the motion is untimely is rejected because the motion was filed within 45 days of service of verification of the responses. Defendant has recently served verified amended responses but those to Nos. 4, 5, 20, 21, and 22 do not fully comply with CCP section 2033.220 (c). Nor are amended responses to Nos. 10, 11, and 12 Code-compliant. Defendant claims to be unable to admit or deny No. 10 because the word "harm" is vague but No. 10 does not contain the word "harm." While defendant objects to Nos. 11 and 12 because the word "harm" is not defined, he cites no authority that this use of a commonly understood word makes the requests so "vague and ambiguous" he is unable to admit or deny them. Moreover, Nos. 11 and 12 specify that the word "harm" is used as in CACI 430. As is clear to anyone who litigates cases stemming from negligence, the word "harm" is used in standard jury instructions. (See Cal. Civil Jury Instructions (CACI) No. 400 approving use of the word "harm" throughout the jury instructions.) If defendant cannot, after reasonable inquiry, admit these requests, or if he in fact denies them, he may so state. What he cannot do is neither admit nor deny the requests, and rely on invalid objections for doing so. The motion to compel is accordingly granted as to Nos. 4, 5, 10, 11, 12, 20, 21, and 22. Defendant shall serve further verified responses within 10 days of service of notice of entry of this order. Plaintiff's request to re-open discovery to conduct further written discovery and a deposition of defendant is denied. Sanctions are awarded against defendant in the amount of $2,000. (end of tenative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ROSS H. MOORE VS. UNICORN CLUB, LTD. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 9, CROSS DEFENDANT ROSS MOORE's MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT. Plaintiff Ross H. Moore's special motion to strike Defendant Unicorn Club LTD.'s cross-complaint is denied. Courts undergo a two-step analysis of Anti-SLAPP motions which involves evaluating (1) whether the moving party can demonstrate that the challenged claims arise out of the moving party's protected petitioning activity; and if so (2) whether the party bringing the claims can demonstrate that those claims have some level of minimal merit such that they have a reasonable probability of success. (CCP §425.16; Navellier v. Sletten (2002) 29 Cal.4th 82, 88.) Plaintiff's motion fails to satisfy the first step of the analysis. Plaintiff has failed to show that Defendant's cross-complaint principally arises out of Plaintiff's protected activity in suing to recover allegedly unused vacation time. While Plaintiff's filing and prosecution of his lawsuit for unpaid wages is protected petitioning activity, Defendant's cross-complaint arises out of Plaintiff's alleged misconduct as a director, unprotected activity that occurred before Plaintiff brought his lawsuit. (See Park v. Bd. of Trustees (2017) 2 Cal.5th 1057, 1060-63 [holding that a claim arises from protected activity only when the moving party's act underlying the claim was itself a protected act].) Courts look to the principal thrust of the challenged causes of action to determine whether they arise from protected activity. (Central Valley Hospitalists v. Dignity Health (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 203, 216-17.) The principal thrust of the claims against Plaintiff are that he engaged in misconduct and breached his fiduciary duties as a director of Unicorn Club before he commenced his lawsuit in his capacity as a former employee of the company in March 2018. (See Cross-Complaint 23, 28, 32(c), 36-38; Trilogy at Glen Ivy Maintenance Assn. v. Shea Homes, Inc. (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 361, 369, 372 [finding the genesis of counterclaims based on breaches of fiduciary duties occurring prior to commencement of initial litigation are not based on protected activity]; Baharian-Mehr v. Smith (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 265, 272 [finding cherry-picked allegations about wage and hour litigation do not constitute the overall thrust of claims based on mismanagement and breach of fiduciary duties].) The discussion in the cross-complaint of Plaintiff's alleged nonuse of vacation and the potential liability this created for Defendant merely provided context to inform the court of the timing of when Defendant learned of the potential liability. (See, e.g., Cross-Complaint 15, 18.) "If the mention of protected activity is only incidental to a cause of action based essentially on nonprotected activity, then the anti-SLAPP statute does not apply." (Baharian-Mehr, 189 Cal.App.4th at 272; Gaynor v. Bulen (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 864, 880 ["The litigation activities . . . would provide evidence of the alleged breaches of fiduciary duty, but the filing of these petitions were not necessary to establish this portion of the breach of fiduciary duty claim."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: ROSS H. MOORE VS. UNICORN CLUB, LTD. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 9, CROSS DEFENDANT ROSS MOORE MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT. Plaintiff Ross H. Moore's special motion to strike Defendant Great Works, Inc.'s cross-complaint is denied. Courts undergo a two-step analysis of anti-SLAPP motions which involves evaluating (1) whether the moving party can demonstrate that the challenged claims arise out of the moving party's protected petitioning activity; and if so (2) whether the party bringing the claims can demonstrate that those claims have some level of minimal merit such that they have a reasonable probability of success. (CCP §425.16; Navellier v. Sletten (2002) 29 Cal.4th 82, 88.) Plaintiff's motion fails to satisfy the first step of the analysis. Plaintiff has failed to show that Defendant's cross-complaint arises out of Plaintiff's protected activity in suing to recover allegedly unused vacation time. While Plaintiff's filing and prosecution of his lawsuit for unpaid wages is protected petitioning activity, Defendant's cross-complaint arises out of Plaintiff's alleged misconduct as a director, unprotected activity that occurred before Plaintiff brought his lawsuit. (See Park v. Bd. of Trustees (2017) 2 Cal.5th 1057, 1060-63 [holding that a claim arises from protected activity only when the moving party's act underlying the claim was itself a protected act].) Courts look to the principal thrust of the challenged causes of action to determine whether they arise from protected activity. (Central Valley Hospitalists v. Dignity Health (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 203, 216-17.) The principal thrust of the claims against Plaintiff is that he engaged in misconduct and breached his fiduciary duties as a director of Great Works before he commenced his lawsuit in his capacity as a former employee of the company in March 2018. (See Cross-Complaint 23, 28, 32(c), 36-38; Trilogy at Glen Ivy Maintenance Assn. v. Shea Homes, Inc. (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 361, 369, 372 [finding the genesis of counterclaims based on breaches of fiduciary duties occurring prior to commencement of initial litigation are not based on protected activity]; Baharian-Mehr v. Smith (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 265, 272 [finding cherry-picked allegations about wage and hour litigation do not constitute the overall thrust of claims based on mismanagement and breach of fiduciary duties].) The discussion in the cross-complaint of Plaintiff's alleged nonuse of vacation and the potential liability this created for Defendant merely provided context to inform the court of the timing of when Defendant learned of the potential liability. (See, e.g., Cross-Complaint 15, 18.) "If the mention of protected activity is only incidental to a cause of action based essentially on nonprotected activity, then the anti-SLAPP statute does not apply." (Baharian-Mehr, 189 Cal.App.4th at 272; Gaynor v. Bulen (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 864, 880 ["The litigation activities . . . would provide evidence of the alleged breaches of fiduciary duty, but the filing of these petitions were not necessary to establish this portion of the breach of fiduciary duty claim."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Great Works is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MUSA JARADIE VS. YIHANG ZHANG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Catherien Walsh In Support Thereof; [Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, March 5, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT YIHANG ZHANG'S Motion To Compel Answers To Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Melinda Derish, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's unopposed motion is granted. Sanctions are awarded against plaintiff in the amount of $300, to be paid by April 5, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to mtderish@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TOUR-SARKISSIAN LAW OFFICES, LLP VS. CHUNG W CHAN JR. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant For Order To Show Cause Re Contempt Against Plaintiff, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities, And Declaration Of Defendant
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 10, DEFENDANT CHUNG CHAN's Motion Of Defendant For Order To Show Cause Re Contempt Against Plaintiff. Defendant Chung W. Chan's motion for an order to show cause re contempt against Plaintiff Tour-Sarkissian Law Offices, LLP is denied. On January 8, 2019, the Court granted Mr. Chan's motion to compel arbitration and stayed this action pending the completion of the arbitration proceedings. The Court has "no jurisdiction to dismiss an arbitration proceeding for failure to prosecute it in a reasonably diligent fashion." (Brock v. Kaiser Foundation Hospitals (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1790, 1793.) "Appellate courts have . . . routinely rejected parties' efforts to have courts overstep their limited jurisdiction in cases that are stayed pending binding contractual arbitration." (Optimal Markets, Inc. v. Salant (2013) 221 Cal.App.4th 912, 924.) Thus, the Court lacks the authority to hold Plaintiff in contempt for purportedly delaying or failing to comply with its order compelling arbitration. (Blake v. Ecker (2001) 93 Cal.App.4th 728, 737 [once the trial court stayed plaintiff's civil action, defendants' only avenue for redress when plaintiff failed to timely prosecute the arbitration was in the arbitration proceeding, not through court order].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTION GLOVER ET AL VS. HALLMARK AVIATION SERVICES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Individual, Non-Class Arbitration And Stay
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, March 05, 2019, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANT HALLMARK AVIATION SERVICES, L.P.'s Motion To Compel Individual, Non-Class Arbitration And Stay. Hallmark Aviation Services, L.P.'s motion to compel arbitration of the individual, non-class claims asserted by plaintiffs Christine Glover, Melinda Soriano, Maria Vicedor, and Tiffany Gomez is granted, and the action is stayed pending the completion of arbitration. Hallmark Aviation has met its burden to show by a preponderance of the evidence that plaintiffs electronically signed the New Hire Policy Acknowledgement Form by completing an onboarding process which Plaintiffs accessed through a unique user name and password provided to them through their personal email accounts. (See Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1062 [declaration authenticating electronic signature established existence of agreement to arbitrate]; Cal. Civ. Code §1633.9(a); Kim Decl. 4-6, 11, Exhs. E-J). By signing and agreeing to the New Hire Policy Acknowledgement Form, Plaintiffs acknowledged that they had reviewed and understood each separately referenced document, including Hallmark Aviation's Alternative Dispute Resolution Policy, a separate document accessible for review and download online. "The fact that [plaintiff] either chose not to read or take the time to understand these provisions is legally irrelevant." (Harris v. TAP Worldwide, LLC (2016) 248 Cal.App.4th 373, 383.) The Court's full tentative ruling is sent by email to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
